title: The Man at the ATM
content_story:
It was raining hard when I set out to withdraw cash, the kind of rain that makes you question whether the errand was worth leaving the house for. I hadn't decided yet. I would, in about twenty minutes, decide that it was — for reasons that had nothing to do with money.

Walking up to the SBI ATM, I noticed a man loitering nearby. He was, at a glance, nearly twice my age and, just as strikingly, about half my height — a combination that struck me first as comic. He kept peering into the ATM booth, ducking his head in and pulling it back out, like a man checking if a room was occupied before deciding whether to enter. I didn't think much of it. Rain does strange things to people's patience.

The SBI machine had an Out of Order sign taped to its screen. I sighed, the particular sigh reserved for small Indian-Friday-afternoon defeats, and walked to the Bank of Baroda ATM next door.

I hadn't noticed the man following me until I was already inside, card in hand. He opened the door, stuck his head in, and asked — a little too eagerly — if money could be withdrawn here. It was an odd way to phrase the question, but I assumed he'd seen the SBI machine's out-of-order sign too and was checking if this one shared the same fate. I said yes. His face changed instantly — that particular brightening of relief — and he stepped in without waiting for an invitation.

I felt the mild irritation you feel when a stranger stands slightly too close in a small space. I let it go. I'd pulled out my Bank of Baroda card out of habit, then remembered I meant to use my SBI account instead, and switched cards. He noticed — he was noticing everything, I'd realize later — and asked if his SBI card would work in a Bank of Baroda machine. I said yes. Then he asked if I could help him withdraw some cash.

This, I want to be clear, did not alarm me. It has happened to me more times than I can count — someone unfamiliar with a machine, someone who doesn't read English comfortably, someone who simply distrusts the beeping metal box that holds their money. Helping a stranger use an ATM is such an ordinary kindness in India that it barely registers as one.

So I withdrew my own cash first, aware of him watching every keystroke with an attention that, in hindsight, was unusual — the way a student watches a teacher solve a problem they're about to be tested on. Then he produced his card: new, shiny, obviously fresh out of a bank envelope. He wanted ₹500.

And then he told me why. It was Rath Yatra, he said. His children had been begging him all morning to take them to see the chariots, and he'd realized, with the particular embarrassment of a father caught short, that he had no cash on him — not even for the festival his own kids were excited about. So here he was, in the rain, chasing down a working ATM.

I began the transaction for him. The machine asked for his PIN. I looked away — an instinct, the small courtesy of not watching a stranger's fingers near their PIN pad. He started reciting the digits aloud as he entered them. "Five —" I heard, and something in me flinched. I looked back and said, quickly, no, no, no — this screen wants the PIN, not the amount. I cleared it and told him to try again.

He laughed, a little confused, and asked — half to me, half to himself — whether Bank of Baroda machines ask for the PIN before the amount. (SBI, I explained, asks for the amount first.) It was a strangely touching question, a man trying to make sense of a machine that behaves differently depending on which bank owns it, as if the two ATMs were dialects of the same language.

I looked away again while he entered his PIN. He recited it aloud once more — "two... zero... two... six" — and I sighed, half from the rain, half from the faint discomfort of a stranger loudly narrating a secret I hadn't asked to know. The transaction went through. I handed him his ₹500 note and stepped outside quickly, not lingering, the way you don't linger after a small act of goodwill you'd rather not make a scene of.

He followed me out and walked to his bicycle while I crossed to my car. I was standing by the gate, half-looking at my phone, when I heard him call out.

He wanted me to know something. He'd opened that account only this year, he said — that's why his PIN was 2026. He found this hilarious. He said I was a "happy go lucky" person, laughed loudly, and rode off into the rain on his bicycle.

I stood there for a moment, turning it over. Somewhere in the last ten minutes, I had — without quite noticing — built a small case against this man in my head: the loitering, the following, the too-keen watching, the urgency around a stranger's PIN. None of it had been sinister. It had been a father, a festival, a new bank account, and a man who found his own PIN funny enough to share with someone he'd known for four minutes.
content_reflection:
What stayed with me wasn't the transaction. It was the gap between the story I was quietly assembling and the story that was actually happening.

I hadn't decided he was dangerous. I hadn't even decided he was suspicious, not consciously. But somewhere between the loitering, the following, and the too-close watching, I'd started keeping score without meaning to — filing away small oddities as potential evidence. When the ending turned out warm instead of dark, I felt almost caught out, like I'd been quietly rehearsing a version of events that never arrived.

It made me realize how much of "street smarts" is really just narrative-building under pressure — and how rarely we notice we're doing it until the story resolves the other way.
content_picture:
This exact sequence — a stranger who follows you from a failed ATM to a working one, watches your PIN entry closely, then asks for "help" with his own card — is also the precise shape of one of India's most common ATM frauds. That's worth unpacking in detail, because the line between this story and that scam is thinner than most people assume.

HOW THE FRAUDULENT VERSION USUALLY WORKS
1. The approach — a stranger loiters near a working or out-of-order ATM, watching for someone heading in alone.
2. The follow — they tail the target to a second ATM once the first is found non-functional, banking on the target's guard being down after the inconvenience.
3. The "help me" ask — they request help operating the machine, leaning on the very real goodwill Indians extend to people who seem unfamiliar with technology.
4. The distraction — while the target is engaged (often mid-transaction, attention split between two cards), the scammer creates a verbal distraction: a sob story, a loud aside, a sudden question.
5. The swap or the shoulder-surf — either a similar-looking card gets swapped for the target's during a moment of distraction, or the scammer memorizes/records the target's PIN for use with a cloned card later.
6. The exit — the scammer leaves quickly, citing urgency, so the target has no time to double-check their card or balance before the scammer is gone.

A scam and a genuine act of kindness look identical for roughly 90% of the interaction. The only real differences — intent and outcome — are invisible in the moment.

FIVE CHECKPOINTS THAT ACTUALLY PROTECT YOU
- Never let a second card enter the ATM slot while yours is still inside or on-screen. This is the highest-risk moment for card swaps — fully close your own transaction (card ejected, screen back to idle) before touching anyone else's card.

- Don't let anyone recite their PIN aloud, and don't enter someone's PIN for them. Sound is a side-channel — a spoken PIN can be picked up by a hidden phone, an accomplice, or simple eavesdropping. Guide verbally ("enter your four digits now") without ever hearing or touching the actual numbers.

- Treat close watching as a neutral signal, not a reassuring one. Genuine tech-unfamiliarity and scam reconnaissance look identical. Complete your own transaction fully before engaging further, regardless of how the attention reads.

- Be suspicious of anyone who follows you between two ATMs. A legitimate cash need rarely requires tailing a specific stranger to a second machine — this is a stronger red flag than anything that happens inside the second ATM.

- Check your balance or mini-statement before leaving if anything felt off. It costs thirty seconds and catches a swap in the same visit instead of three days later.

WHAT INDIAN BANKS ACTUALLY RECOMMEND
- Cover the keypad with your free hand whenever entering a PIN, regardless of who's present.
- Never accept "help" from a stranger for your own transaction, even just pointing at buttons.
- Report a lost, stolen, or swapped card immediately — most banks, including SBI and Bank of Baroda, allow instant blocking via app, SMS banking, or a toll-free helpline.
- Prefer ATMs with visible CCTV and a security guard, especially at odd hours or low-footfall locations.
- Set SMS/app transaction alerts so unauthorized withdrawals are flagged within seconds, not days.

THE UNCOMFORTABLE MIDDLE GROUND
None of this means you should stop helping people at ATMs. Refusing every request would fail the genuine cases — the elderly, first-time account holders, anyone intimidated by the machine — far more often than it would stop a scam. The real takeaway is narrower: complete your own transaction fully and privately before helping with anyone else's, never let two cards overlap in the same window of time, and never let a PIN travel through the air as sound. Kindness and caution aren't actually in tension — they only feel that way in the moment, because the moment never comes labeled.
content_questions: Was the narrator right to be quietly suspicious, or did the suspicion say more about the narrator than the stranger?
Should you ever help a stranger enter their own PIN?
Where should kindness end and caution begin at a shared public terminal like an ATM?
Have you ever built a "case" against someone in your head that turned out to be completely wrong?
Is India's culture of helping strangers with banking machines a strength or a liability in the digital age?
content_behind: Written the same evening this happened — heavy Rath Yatra rain, an SBI ATM out of order, and an unplanned detour that turned into this piece. The details (the PIN, the height difference, the "happy go lucky" line) are kept exactly as they happened; nothing here is embellished for effect.
